verdict_reasoning: C3 is a secreted plasma glycoprotein with no TM domain, GPI anchor, or outer-leaflet lipidation at any lifecycle stage. Its cleavage product C3b deposits covalently via a reactive thioester onto pathogen/foreign surfaces as an opsonin — this is deposition onto target surfaces, not stable anchoring to the C3-expressing human cell's own plasma membrane. Anti-C3 therapeutics (e.g., pegcetacoplan) target the circulating/soluble pool, not a cell-surface-resident form. Pre-no checklist: no cell-state-induced ecto-pool on human cells documented; no germline/developmental surface display; not a lysosomal TM protein; no documented PM cycling minority pool on human cells; no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering to a human cell surface. The covalent thioester chemistry deposits C3b onto foreign/target surfaces — the recruiter is the foreign activating surface, not a human-cell TM partner. Verdict: secreted_only.
